engineering.translation: This shloka closes the karma-yoga argument and pivots the chapter. The buddhi that was previously perplexed by the divergent teachings of the Veda — Shankara's reading — must come to stand niścalā (unmoving when in samādhi) and acalā (unwavering once unmoving). When that layered condition is met, yoga is attained. The verse is grammatically forward-looking: tadā yogam avāpsyasi — THEN you will attain yoga. It is also the explicit setup for Arjuna's question at 2.54 ('how does such a person speak, sit, walk?') which begins the sthita-prajña block. The engineering reading: the operator whose decision-making no longer oscillates with each new framework release has met the closing condition. Across one quarter, three new architecture proposals land; her judgment does not move with the last one she heard. Her buddhi is niścalā — not pulled by external claims — and acalā — not internally re-litigating once the pulls have been resisted. The next twenty verses describe what such an operator looks like in practice.
engineering.concrete_scenario: A staff engineer ten years in. Across the last quarter her team has fielded three architecture proposals, each from a credible engineer. First: 'we should move to a service mesh; Istio gives us mTLS and observability for free.' Second: 'we should rewrite the auth layer in Rust for memory safety.' Third: 'we should adopt a vector database for the AI feature pipeline; pgvector won't scale.' Three years ago each would have produced a multi-week roadmap perturbation: a design doc on Notion, two architecture review meetings, a Slack thread pulling the team's focus for a week. This quarter, each proposal is met with the same shape. She reads it. She maps the claim against actual constraints — current Kubernetes complexity, current Postgres footprint, current AI feature volume. She replies in two paragraphs: here is what would be true if we adopted this; here is the cost; here is the trigger that would make us reconsider. The team's sprint plan does not bend. None are adopted this quarter; one is filed for re-evaluation when the AI pipeline crosses a measurable threshold. The verse names this as the closing condition for attaining yoga. What 2.54 onwards describes — how the sthita-prajña speaks, sits, walks — the next twenty verses begin to spell out, and her two-paragraph replies, her unbent sprint plan, her trigger-based re-evaluation calendar are the operational shape of the buddhi that stood niścalā and acalā.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader reads niścalā and acalā as 'rigid' or 'closed to change'. The verse is naming a settled state, not a defended one. The senior in the example DOES evaluate each proposal carefully and DOES reserve one for re-evaluation under a measurable trigger. What is absent is the perturbation. An engineer who concludes from this verse that they should pre-commit to never adopting new architectures has confused the settled buddhi with a defensive crouch. The diagnostic: when a new framework or pattern crosses an actual decision threshold (real constraint, real measurement), does the engineer adopt it cleanly? If yes, the buddhi is settled. If no, what looks like steadiness is reflexive contrarianism.
engineering.counter_example: When a genuine architectural pivot is required — the Postgres setup is no longer scalable, the auth provider has announced end-of-life, the AI pipeline has crossed the threshold she set — the verse does not require the engineer to remain unmoved. niścalā is unmoving with respect to the noise of divergent claims (śruti-vipratipannā), not with respect to actual situational change. The buddhi the verse names reads situational change accurately and adopts the necessary pivot; it does not bend with each unrelated proposal.
engineering.implication: Count the architecture proposals that crossed your team last quarter versus those that produced actual roadmap change. Subtract those where situational reality required the change. The remainder is how much the team's buddhi is still pulled by śruti-vipratipannā.
engineering.quotable_line: Three architecture proposals landed; the sprint plan did not bend. That is the closing condition the verse names.

iterations.mutation: v0 generation: source pack triangulated across vedabase.io (Prabhupada) + holy-bhagavad-gita.org (Mukundananda) + gitasupersite.iitk.ac.in (Devanagari) + bhagavad-gita.us (Shankara/Ramanuja excerpts) + GRETIL critical edition (Bhg_02.053). CRITICAL CARE per task spec: this verse closes the karma-yoga argument AND pivots to the sthita-prajña block. Engineering analog explicitly foreshadows the sthita-prajña without copying — 'what 2.54 onwards describes is what the next twenty verses begin to spell out'. Concrete scenario: staff engineer fielding three architecture proposals (Istio service mesh, Rust auth rewrite, vector database) where the buddhi-settled response is two-paragraph evaluation with trigger-based re-evaluation; the sprint plan does not bend. Layered closing condition preserved (niścalā = no external pull, acalā = no internal doubt after pull resisted). Counter-example carves out genuine situational change. Falsifiability handles the misreading 'rigid'. Named tools: Kubernetes, Postgres, Istio, Notion, Slack, pgvector. Tags: first-principles, judgment-under-uncertainty, scope-discipline, roadmap-conviction.

Single mutation: trim concrete_scenario, counter_example, and implication by ~50 words across three small edits to clear the 11.1 word cap. Specifically: removed redundant 'each from a credible engineer / each of these / shape of response' phrasings, dropped 'genuinely' twice, condensed the implication. The pivot-foreshadowing language ('what 2.54 onwards describes') and the niścalā/acalā doctrinal pair are preserved intact. Engineering substance untouched.
